posts.summary: Fernando Pérez traces the evolution of IPython and Jupyter from a 259-line afternoon hack to a global platform for interactive scientific computing. He explains the core architectural decisions—a language-agnostic protocol over ZeroMQ with JSON encoding, and a web-native interface—that enabled Jupyter to support over 100 programming language kernels and scale from laptops to supercomputers. He demonstrates JupyterLab's modular extensibility, including drag-and-drop cells, GitHub integration, GeoJSON viewers, and interactive widgets. Pérez makes a passionate case for open-source tools in science on ethical, community, and epistemological grounds, arguing that proprietary tools in the path of scientific discovery are fundamentally broken. He showcases reproducibility efforts through Binder and the LIGO gravitational wave analysis, and describes Berkeley's Data 8 course scaling to 700 students as a model for data science education.

posts.key_insight: By abstracting interactive computing into a language-agnostic protocol and embracing the web platform, Jupyter transformed from a personal Python hack into infrastructure that bridges the gap between human thinking and computation at every scale, from education to gravitational wave discovery.

posts.summary: Fernando Pérez traces the evolution of IPython and Jupyter from a personal terminal tool in 2001 to a global platform for interactive computing used by scientists, educators, and industry. He emphasizes the ethical imperative of building open tools, particularly for researchers in developing countries who cannot afford proprietary software licenses. The talk highlights Jupyter's architecture inspired by web protocols, its expansion to support over 100 programming languages, and its role in landmark scientific achievements like the LIGO gravitational wave detection. Pérez also discusses the transformative impact of Jupyter on education at UC Berkeley, where Data 8 and Data 100 courses are reaching over half the campus. He closes with reflections on representation, mentorship, and the value of non-academic career paths.

posts.key_insight: Open tools for science are not merely a technical preference but an ethical imperative — they democratize access, enable reproducibility, and build human communities that proprietary software structurally cannot.

posts.summary: Fernando Pérez delivers a keynote on the sustainable development of Jupyter and open scientific tools, tracing the project's evolution from a 260-line Python script to a multi-language platform used by millions. He demonstrates JupyterLab's modular architecture, JupyterHub's deployment capabilities, and Binder's one-click reproducibility. He shares how he taught reproducible data science practices to undergraduates at UC Berkeley, turning them into everyday habits rather than afterthoughts. The talk concludes with a frank examination of the tensions between open source sustainability, academic incentives, and industry's extractive relationship with open source communities.

posts.summary: Fernando Pérez presents UC Berkeley's massive adoption of Jupyter as infrastructure for data science education as a case study of institutional transformation. The Data 8 course enrolls 1,300 students per lecture and could reach 50% of all undergraduates annually, powered entirely by Jupyter notebooks and cloud-based JupyterHub infrastructure at roughly $2 per student per week. The ecosystem includes connector courses, drop-in modules for existing courses, and a new data science major drawing students from 73 different majors with improved gender diversity. Pérez emphasizes this was built with minimal resources by a team of mostly undergraduates, and frames the broader question of what happens when computational literacy becomes as commonplace as email across an entire university.

posts.summary: Fernando Pérez presents a comprehensive vision for reproducible scientific research, drawing from his experience teaching STAT 159 at UC Berkeley and the development of Project Jupyter's Binder infrastructure. He traces the intellectual lineage of reproducibility from Claerbout and Donoho in the 1990s through to modern tools, arguing that reproducibility must be a daily practice rather than a publication-time afterthought. Using a real-world case study of a Norwegian wind patterns paper, he demonstrates how even well-intentioned open science efforts suffer from bit rot. He showcases Binder as a tool that packages computational environments into one-click executable links, exemplified by the LIGO gravitational waves discovery notebooks. The talk emphasizes lowering barriers to access for scientists worldwide, particularly those without institutional resources.

posts.summary: Fernando Pérez shares his personal journey from growing up in Medellín, Colombia to becoming a professor at UC Berkeley, tracing how a PhD crisis led him to create IPython and eventually co-found Project Jupyter. He explains his four motivations for open source work—ethical fairness in access, community building, epistemological integrity in science, and technical passion—while emphasizing that personal crisis was the actual catalyst. He describes how the scientific Python ecosystem grew from justifying its existence in 2008 to powering Nobel Prize-winning research and being adopted by major tech companies. Pérez highlights Berkeley's massive data science education initiative reaching 1,300 freshmen and potentially half the campus, and closes with advice on choosing mentors carefully and rejecting the stigma around non-academic careers.

posts.summary: In this TEDxBerkeley talk, Fernando Pérez shares his personal journey from growing up in violence-torn Medellín, Colombia to building IPython and Jupyter, tools that transformed scientific computing. He describes how getting fired by his PhD advisor inadvertently led him to create IPython as a conversational computing tool, which grew into the Jupyter project through open community collaboration. Pérez emphasizes that the open source model enabled unprecedented scientific achievements, including the LIGO gravitational wave discovery, and revolutionized education at UC Berkeley through the Foundations of Data Science course. He argues that open, collaborative development is humanity's best chance at ensuring the data revolution benefits society broadly rather than enriching only a few.

posts.summary: Fernando Pérez traces his unconventional path from theoretical physics to creating IPython and co-founding Jupyter, arguing that open source scientific computing tools are far more than software — they are infrastructure requiring community governance, professional management, and sustainable funding. He demonstrates Jupyter's impact through examples ranging from Berkeley's massive data science courses to Nobel Prize-winning gravitational wave research, while highlighting the crisis that builders of foundational open source tools face in academia. Pérez makes a compelling economic case that investing just 0.1% of the federal R&D budget ($200 million) in open source infrastructure would yield enormous returns, but emphasizes the hard organizational problem of spending that money well. posts.summary: Fernando Pérez argues that open source software in science is far more than code — it encompasses services, standards, protocols, and most critically, human communities. Despite powering trillions of dollars in industry value and underpinning the most important scientific results of the decade, these projects remain chronically underfunded and their creators face hostile academic incentive structures. posts.summary: Fernando Pérez presents the Jupyter ecosystem's growing role in earth sciences and geoscience research, framing it within the broader challenge of modern scientific computing. He argues that as science becomes more complex, individual researchers cannot be expected to master theory, data engineering, and cloud infrastructure simultaneously, and that open-source tools like Jupyter, Binder, and Pangeo help bridge these gaps. He details how Jupyter evolved from a small Python script into a massive collaborative project with standardized protocols, governance structures, and industry partnerships. The talk also highlights Jupyter's impact on data science education at Berkeley and reproducible science (LIGO, Event Horizon Telescope), while candidly addressing the tension between open-source contribution and traditional academic incentive structures.

Beyond software code, impactful open-source projects require investment in content, services, protocols/standards, governance, and community — the software itself is often the least important part

posts.summary: In this PyCon Colombia 2020 keynote, Fernando Pérez traces his journey from studying physics at Universidad de Antioquia in Medellín to creating IPython and co-founding Jupyter, emphasizing how open source communities transformed scientific computing. We're not teaching students with toys. We're not teaching with a toy thing that they'll have to abandon when they go out into the real world — we're teaching them literally with the same tools that researchers wrote for their own problems.

When we were creating all of this, they told us explicitly: stop doing this, this is a waste of time, this is worthless, this isn't real work. And knowing that one of the most important results in physics in recent years was partly done with this — that is incredibly satisfying.
The project is the people, the community. It's not two or three people, it's not the code, but a team of people.
Taking the time to go from free software to a series of documented, abstracted ideas defined as an open standard allows an entire ecosystem to grow.
posts.key_insight: The lasting impact of open source scientific software comes not from the code itself but from layering open standards, governance, and inclusive community on top of it — transforming tools into ecosystems that serve both cutting-edge research and universal education.

posts.summary: In this interview at PyCon Colombia 2020, Fernando Pérez recounts the origin of IPython as a doctoral distraction in 2001, born from merging three independent projects by scientists who all wanted interactive computing tools. He explains the evolution from IPython to Jupyter, motivated by the need for a language-agnostic name as the notebook system expanded to support R, Julia, and other languages. Pérez discusses the Jupyter ecosystem's future directions including JupyterLab, JupyterHub, JupyterBook, Binder, and Voilà. He emphasizes that building open source community requires far more than writing code — it demands human communication, collaboration, and welcoming practices. He also argues that data scientists must deeply understand their problem domains and grapple with the ethical implications of deploying automated decision-making systems.
key_points: IPython began in 2001 as a collaborative merger of three independent projects by scientists who needed interactive computing tools for exploration, not just software development

Jupyter was split from IPython because the notebook architecture was language-agnostic by design, and the name captures connections to Julia-Python-R, astronomy, and the Galilean spirit of scientists building their own tools

The Jupyter ecosystem is expanding into modular scientific environments (JupyterLab), cloud-scale collaboration (JupyterHub/Pangeo), interactive publishing (JupyterBook/Binder), and visual dashboards (Voilà)
Community success depends more on human practices — communication, welcoming newcomers, collaboration — than on code quality alone
Data scientists bear ethical responsibility for their systems' impacts on society and cannot hide behind 'the algorithm decided' as an excuse
money_quotes: Le dije a mi asesora que le iba a trabajar una tardecita a esto y, 20 años más tarde, ahí sigo metido.
Lo que crea comunidad no es el código, la comunidad está hecha de seres humanos y lo que crea la comunidad es la comunicación, es la colaboración, es la participación, es el diálogo.

Galileo era un científico que hacía sus propias herramientas, Galileo tuvo que inventar el telescopio para descubrir las lunas de Júpiter y nosotros somos científicos que nos gustaba hacer herramientas.

Yo no voy a decir que otros lenguajes no sirven o que son inferiores, ni muchísimo menos, entonces tenemos un nombre y un proyecto que capturaron el espíritu de la colaboración en la comunidad científica.
Los algoritmos los escribimos seres humanos, los desplegamos en la práctica de seres humanos, la responsabilidad del impacto que eso tiene sobre la vida de la gente sigue siendo nuestra.

No se trata de agarrar cada herramienta como cogiendo martillos de una pared a ver con cuál le puede dar el clavo, sino realmente entender cuál es el nexo entre las preguntas del dominio, cómo fueron adquiridos los datos y qué herramientas metodológicas se pueden usar.
Corrimos con la suerte de que teníamos un proyecto que resolvía un problema en el momento adecuado.
Mucha gente que llega al mundo del open source llega pensando que se trata de programar, pero resulta que lo que crea comunidad no es el código.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable open source communities are built not by code alone but by deliberate human practices of communication, collaboration, and welcoming participation — and the tools they produce carry ethical responsibilities that their creators must own.

posts.summary: Fernando Pérez presents a comprehensive overview of the Jupyter ecosystem at NeuroHackademy 2020, framing open source software as far more than code—encompassing services, open standards, community governance, and human collaboration. He demonstrates JupyterLab's extensible architecture through live demos ranging from local data exploration to processing 1.6 terabytes of climate data on NCAR supercomputers using Dask. He details how Binder enables one-click reproducible science, citing examples from the LIGO gravitational wave discovery to Python language design collaboration. The talk also covers his Berkeley course on reproducibility, emphasizing that tools represent ideas and that version control, dependency management, and testing should be daily scientific practices, not afterthoughts.
key_points: Open source scientific projects transcend code: services like NBViewer and Binder, open standards like the Jupyter protocol, and community governance are equally critical layers that enable broad impact

The Jupyter protocol's language-agnostic design enables an ecosystem of interoperable tools—just as HTTP enabled services beyond web browsing, the Jupyter protocol enables interactive computing beyond Python notebooks

JupyterLab's extensibility allows domain scientists to build custom interfaces (like the Fruit Fly Brain Lab or NIfTI viewer) while still accessing the broader ecosystem, mirroring how Python's general-purpose nature lets scientists focus on domain tools

Binder transforms reproducibility from a theoretical ideal into a practical one-click reality, powering everything from LIGO's Nobel Prize-winning open science to Python language design discussions to data journalism

The next frontier in open access battles with publishers is not PDFs but computation, code, and data—and the scientific community must maintain a strong stake rather than ceding control to commercial providers
money_quotes: Impactful software happens also when communities take the time to take a step back and ask which ideas their implementation represents, and to pull those ideas away from the implementation.
I want to make sure that it's absolutely crystal clear to everyone here that none of this is my work and everything I'll be talking about today is the outcome of team effort.

It always bothers me that our culture and our incentive mechanisms are so overly obsessed with the notion of individual heroes and make it difficult often to create healthy open teams that are really genuinely collaborative.

Back when I was doing my PhD in physics, I had to justify to everyone why I was using Python. Every talk that I tried to give had a bunch of slides of basically advocacy, because this wasn't considered to be real work. And 15 years later, to see one of the most important discoveries in physics done with tools that come from our community was one of the most satisfying moments of my professional career.
I think of version control as the toothbrush and dental floss of computing. This is something that you should do every day.
Reproducibility enables collaboration. Making something like this easily reproducible with one click makes it easy for a community to collaborate rather than in the abstract.

Those PDFs are in a sense last year's battle because the real future of these battles with these very large commercial entities around open access to science is going to be not around PDFs but around the rest of the research which is the computation and the codes and the data.

It's not just about making the work reproducible from a purely technical standpoint that yes, the code is there if you know how to put it together, but it's to make that practical for working everyday scientists who are not interested and should not be interested in becoming full-time software engineers.
posts.key_insight: Open source scientific software achieves its greatest impact not through code alone, but through the layered ecosystem of open standards, community governance, accessible services, and reproducibility tools that empower domain scientists to collaborate and share their work without becoming software engineers.

posts.summary: Fernando Pérez presents his approach to teaching reproducible research practices at UC Berkeley, describing a course that teaches students version control, testing, continuous integration, and open publishing using Jupyter tools. He frames open source scientific computing as more than software—it's an ecosystem of tools, ideas, communities, and governance that enables collaborative science. He showcases real-world examples including the LIGO gravitational wave notebooks, the Pangeo geoscience platform, and national-scale infrastructure in Canada, arguing that openness is both an ethical imperative and a practical foundation for tackling global challenges like climate change and disinformation.
key_points: A reproducibility course at Berkeley successfully teaches students from diverse backgrounds a 'playbook' of version control, testing, CI, and open publishing without requiring them to become software engineers
Open source software is more than code—it encompasses services, content, extensible protocols, community governance, and fiscal infrastructure like NumFOCUS
Jupyter's design as an extensible 'operating system for data science' enables domain-specific adaptations like FlyBrain Lab while maintaining shared infrastructure
Berkeley's open data science courses are reaching saturation point where computational literacy is becoming as natural as email for students
Pérez's personal motivation stems from growing up in Colombia, where building with proprietary tools meant excluding people like his former self from participating in science
money_quotes: Version control is kind of the toothbrush of science, right, and the dental floss of computational practices. It's something that you should do. You should use it for everything. You should use it every day.
Building research with open tools was an ethical imperative. Openness meant fairness, it meant access to science and education for someone like I was months before I moved to Colorado.

I wanted to be able to participate in the process of science, even if I was in Colombia. And so once I had access to those resources, I thought, well, if I keep using Mathematica, MATLAB, IDL, I cannot share this work in good conscience with my former mentors or with my former self.
Open source software is more than software. Yes, the software matters, but it turns out that in a nod to kind of the Maslow hierarchy of needs, open source software powers services and content.

I'm not claiming that open science or any of what we've done here is going to fix any of these problems. I'm not that stupid, I'm not that naive. But I hope that these tools and these practices can be a little grain of sand in the pile on the right side of that balance.

A child in my home country in Colombia who has access to the internet can actually open on their phone this link, and they can actually play the real world data from the detection of gravitational waves. I think that is an important change in the culture of science.
I find that far more personally satisfying than the more traditional kind of cutthroat competition that is very typical of traditional academic practices.
If every scientist was doing this, I would be okay with that and they don't need to become full-blown engineers.
posts.key_insight: Open source scientific tools are not just software but an ecosystem of protocols, communities, and governance that transforms science from a consumption model to collaborative creation—and teaching a minimal reproducibility playbook is both viable and sufficient for working scientists.

posts.summary: Fernando Pérez traces his journey from growing up amid violence in Medellín, Colombia to creating IPython and co-founding the Jupyter project. He explains how his inability to afford proprietary scientific software in Colombia, a failed attempt at teaching computational physics with primitive open-source tools, and disillusionment with competitive academic culture drove him to build open, accessible computing tools for science. He demonstrates Jupyter's impact through examples including the LIGO gravitational wave discovery, the first black hole image, and NASA's Mars helicopter, arguing that open-source scientific computing has fundamentally transformed how research and education are conducted.
key_points: The inaccessibility of expensive proprietary scientific software in developing countries like Colombia was a primary motivation for building open-source alternatives
IPython was born from merging collaborative contributions and has evolved over 20 years into the Jupyter ecosystem supporting 100+ programming languages
Open-source scientific Python tools have enabled Nobel Prize-winning research (LIGO gravitational waves), the first black hole image, and NASA's Mars helicopter mission
UC Berkeley's data science courses serve 1,200-1,500 students using Jupyter notebooks with zero-install cloud infrastructure, making computational skills as natural as checking email
Using proprietary black-box software to understand nature is epistemologically inconsistent with the open inquiry that defines science
money_quotes: I didn't believe in the notion that we should understand how nature is put together by using tools that we are not allowed to understand.

The heart of scientific computing is not about writing software for a fixed set of requirements. It's about using the computer as a tool to think, as a tool to explore, as a tool to operate on your data and gradually build not only an understanding but build the questions that you want to ask next.

I will never teach something like this unless we have better tools so that we can focus on the ideas, the science, thinking about physics and computing, rather than the low-level mechanics of systems management.

I quickly discovered that a lot of day-to-day science was more about competition than about collaboration. What I found in the open source community was a bunch of scientists who were genuinely interested in sharing their work with one another.
We're teaching first-semester undergrads with the same exact tools that Nobel Prize-winning research in physics was conducted with, which we think is a phenomenally transformative process.
By having tools that we can open and build and improve upon ourselves, we become producers and creators as well, and therefore participants.

Some of my senior mentors flat out told me: you need to stop doing this, go back to writing physics and math papers, that's what you're good at. Their point was that what you're doing is not real work.
If I have to learn from my undergrads, that is a good day.
posts.key_insight: Open-source scientific computing tools, born from the need for equitable access and genuine collaboration, have grown from a dismissed side project into infrastructure underpinning Nobel Prize research and transforming science education at scale.

posts.summary: Fernando Pérez delivers a deeply personal keynote at SciPy 2021's 20th anniversary, tracing his journey from a crisis-ridden PhD student who created IPython as a procrastination project to a Berkeley professor whose tools helped detect gravitational waves and image black holes. He celebrates the scientific Python community's extraordinary impact while warning that the current model of volunteer-driven open source development is unsustainable. He argues that scientific open source software is foundational public infrastructure—like roads and bridges—that requires systematic federal funding, not just ad hoc philanthropy and weekend volunteering. He calls for dedicating even 1% of the federal R&D budget to properly support the open source tools that underpin modern science.
key_points: IPython and the SciPy ecosystem have enabled landmark scientific achievements including LIGO gravitational wave detection, the first black hole image, and the Mars helicopter telemetry—all built on community tools
The current open source sustainability model is dangerously reliant on volunteer labor, which excludes those who cannot afford to donate nights and weekends, undermining both diversity and resilience
Scientific open source is foundational digital infrastructure analogous to roads and bridges, and should be funded as a public good from the ~$200 billion federal R&D budget
The community still lacks diversity and inclusion despite progress, and building a more inclusive future requires moving beyond the volunteer-only model that favors those with economic privilege
New organizational models like 2I2C, CZI's EOSS program, and NumFOCUS represent promising approaches to sustainable funding, but federal agencies must step up systematically
money_quotes: IPython was kind of an afternoon hack is what I told Anna that I would do. I would kind of go away and play with this language, write a little bit of code and go back to work. Well, it's 20 years later and I'm still here. So take that as the quality of my time estimates.

Relying strictly on volunteers is dangerous because only those who can afford to volunteer can participate. And if you are the breadwinner in your family, if you have children, if you're a minority with economic pressures or family pressures of other kinds, it's going to be much harder for you to enter this community if it's predicated on having spare nights and weekends to build your GitHub portfolio.
Open source is not just magical pixie dust that you spray on things and magically that stuff just happens because somebody does it between midnight and 5 a.m. on Sunday.

I really came, started doing what I thought was geeky techy stuff, but it was this sense of belonging to a group of like-minded people who wanted to collaborate and wanted to work together that changed my entire personal and professional trajectory.
One percent of that budget spent well would make a difference.

The very first time that we got an aircraft on a foreign planet, we got the telemetry from that milestone for humanity down into a Matplotlib window. It really is a remarkable achievement from this community.
I would like to argue that scientific open source is a foundational public good, foundational in the sense that Nadia Egbal kind of phrased the Roads and Bridges report.
It was the generosity of this entire community that has made my career and my life kind of the way it is today possible.
posts.key_insight: Scientific open source software has become foundational infrastructure for modern science, yet its sustainability model—built on volunteer labor and ad hoc funding—is dangerously fragile and must be replaced with systematic public investment.

posts.summary: In this JupyterCon 2023 keynote, Fernando Pérez reflects on 22 years of IPython and Jupyter, tracing the project's origins from his personal crisis as a PhD student in Colorado to its current global impact. He emphasizes that the project was always a community effort, not an individual achievement, and argues against the 'benevolent dictator' model of open source leadership. He highlights the triumph of open science policy, including NASA and the White House declaring 2023 the year of open science, while stressing that true open science must extend beyond free access to include equitable participation and leadership, especially in the Global South. He touches on AI as a transformative 'third voice' in the notebook narrative and urges the community to navigate the tension between innovation and maintaining user trust.
key_points: IPython was a community effort from its very first version, built on code shared by Janko Hauser and Nathan Gray, contradicting the myth of a solo creator
The BDFL governance model is unhealthy for collaborative communities; Jupyter has moved to an executive council, and the hero-worship model should be abandoned
Open science has won at the policy level — NASA's SPD-41A and White House OSTP directives now mandate open science practices for federal funding
True open science means not just free access but equitable participation and leadership, especially for the Global South and Latin America
AI introduces a 'third voice' in the notebook narrative alongside human language and code, and the community must approach it in a human-centric, vendor-agnostic way
money_quotes: Open source saved me in that I got hooked on writing code and it gave me kind of a path forward that kind of tied a number of these motivations.
From the very start, IPython wasn't about me. It was about people sharing their work and doing things together.
I probably wouldn't be here, I might not be personally okay if it hadn't been for open source and for the values and the spirit of collaboration and working together that open source kind of gave me.
That term, not only did it not describe actually what happened in our community in Jupyter, but it's also, I don't think, a good model for thinking about how to grow healthy communities.
What was transformative, at least for me in my life, was the fact that I could participate, I could be an equal actor in the process with others, and ultimately that I could have leadership.

We want to think about the construction of these narratives in ways that are also fair, that are vendor-agnostic, where we're trying to empower the individuals who are working with their tools rather than simply funneling more power back to those who control the infrastructure.
The same ideas that led to our fantastic growth and impact from 10 years ago are not going to be the ones that propel us forward today.
Science should be done openly and not with proprietary, expensive tools that we're not allowed to understand, which to me was epistemologically absurd.
posts.key_insight: True open science is not just about free access to tools but about equitable participation and leadership — everyone must be able to shape the direction, not just consume what others built.
